does: This paper introduces VinQA, a new dataset and task for generating long-form answers to questions about real-world multimodal documents where visual elements like charts, tables, and diagrams are explicitly cited and interleaved within text responses. The authors study two distinct encoding approaches for processing document pages: Page Encoding, which feeds full-page images with bounding boxes marking visual elements, and Modality Encoding, which separately extracts and encodes text and visual elements as individual units for citation. They propose M-GroSE, an evaluation framework that extends GroUSE to assess answer quality across four dimensions including completeness, answer relevancy, and faithfulness, enabling systematic measurement of whether generated responses properly ground visual elements in document content.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in multimodal large language models and document understanding, extending beyond prior research that primarily generates text-only responses from documents. It responds to limitations in existing multimodal document QA benchmarks that fail to leverage the rich visual information present in real documents containing mixed media. The paper connects to earlier work on grounded text generation and evaluation frameworks like GroUSE, adapting these methodologies for the multimodal setting. The visual citation and grounding mechanisms relate to broader research on interpretability and faithfulness in language models, while the encoding method comparisons draw from established approaches in vision-language modeling for handling structured documents.
enables: This work enables future research on more faithful and interpretable multimodal QA systems that properly leverage all document modalities rather than defaulting to text-only responses. The VinQA dataset and M-GroSE evaluation framework provide infrastructure for training and assessing models on this underexplored task of interleaved visual-textual generation. The comparison of encoding strategies offers insights that could optimize how MLLMs process complex real-world documents with intricate layouts. These findings support development of more practical document understanding systems for applications like business intelligence, research synthesis, and automated document summarization where visual elements carry essential information that must be explicitly cited and integrated into generated responses.
